replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
If you're building something, catching issues needs to happen at planning. Altering a house plan when the structure is already partially build is going to increase the complexity of the fix and cost. He should be able to spot a bad idea in the time of an elevator pitch. If something gets an initial greenlight,  he should spend 30 minutes making sure that the plot and tone is fine. He could probably have the entire MCU slate for the next year covered at a high level in a single morning.

With 2 sheets of A4, you could write the plot of a film, and include a character breakdown indicating where the main characters start and where they're left. That way, you assess the plot in isolation, check character consistency and know that the characters have been moved into position for the next work. If the plot points work and you trust the directors to implement a plan that made sense, then you'd hopefully need minor fixes. They're doing complete reshoots over there. They double the budget and are left with crudely sewn together frankenstein's monsters.

text: Bob Iger’s leadership has driven Disney into a perilous decline, squandering its American roots for a reckless global chase that’s left the company teetering on the edge of failure. While billions are dumped into ventures like Shanghai Disney Resort and now this Abu Dhabi venture, domestic parks—once the heart of Disney’s magic—languish with exorbitant prices, crumbling guest experiences, and zero innovation, alienating the loyal U.S. audience that made Disney an icon. Disney+ flounders with pitiful subscriber growth at home, and failed movies, yet Iger keeps pouring resources overseas, a blunder reminiscent of General Motors and Kodak—corporate titans that chased foreign dreams, neglected their core, and collapsed into obscurity. His shortsighted gamble threatens to torch Disney’s cultural legacy and financial might, exposing a CEO more obsessed with global bragging rights than preserving what actually works. author: @BilSande4
text: I love the way everyone acts like the MCU's decline is this great mystery that no one can solve. Is it superhero movie fatigue? Is it too much quantity over quality? Is it too much wokeness? (well, yes) but that's not all it is. The real reason the MCU has seen such a sharp decline in interest is super easy to explain (barely an inconvenience). When I grew up I loved reading comics. Especially Marvel Comics. And the comics I liked reading were things like Spider-Man, Daredevil, Captain America, Iron Man, Hulk, Thor, The Avengers, The X-Men and The Fantastic Four. And when the MCU first started up they used characters like Iron Man, Captain America, Thor, and Hulk, and then got them all together in The Avengers, and then brought in characters like Spider-Man and the GOTG, it was wildly successful beyond anyone's imagination. But that's all over now. They killed Iron Man, they aged Steve Rogers (the only Captain America) out of existence, they gayed up Thor, and ruined The Hulk. With the exception of Spider-Man they basically burned through all of the most popular characters. No one was clamoring for a movie about Black Widow's sister, a c-level (gender swapped) super villain, a discount Captain America, a Russian Super Soldier, and Bucky becoming *The New Avengers. That's why the MCU is failing now, because with few exceptions there are only B and C list characters left to work with. Now that all may change with the coming of the Fantastic Four and it may really change whenever they finally get around to making The X-Men, but until then, the MCU is on life support and it's not a mystery as to why.
